narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to pass on their first try based on academic and background factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 25 (well under the average 36.70), Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (compared to an average of about 5.5), and upper-middle family income—and estimated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction were, in order of influence, your Year 3 decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your family income. Your low Year 3 decile ranking stood out most strongly, signaling to the model weaker performance relative to peers in your school, which heavily tipped the scales toward expecting bar failure. Your LSAT score was the next major driver in the same direction, as its below-average level reinforced concerns about your readiness for the rigors ahead. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing by suggesting some supportive resources not always present in lower brackets. Compared to typical students, who often enter with stronger early and later academic deciles around the middle of their classes and much higher LSATs, your profile aligned more closely with those who struggled on the bar. Your full-time student status is common and didn't sway things much either way. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black men face in competitive fields like law, it doesn't define your potential. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after these improvements could shift the model's view dramatically, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. Keep pushing—you've got this.
